stem: A hospital uses an anti-CCP antibody test to screen for rheumatoid arthritis. The test has a sensitivity of 87% and a specificity of 83%. The prevalence of rheumatoid arthritis in the patient population is estimated to be between 1.7% and 4.4%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has rheumatoid arthritis? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(rheumatoid arthritis | positive test) ranges from 0.0813 to 0.191 as prevalence varies from 1.7% to 4.4%. Point estimate at 3.0% prevalence: 0.137.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.17.
The confidence interval [0.0813, 0.191] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (87%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
